tossups.question: A revolt against a regency headed by a member of this family began when the villagers of Sampford Courtenay objected to a new prayer book. “Fat quails” were sourced from Calais to satisfy the pregnancy cravings of a member of this family. Another member of this family scandalously married the widowed queen Catherine Parr. A woman from this family was the mother of a ruler whose reign saw Robert Kett’s anti-enclosure revolt in Norfolk. That member of this family was depicted meeting her future husband at this family’s seat of Wulfhall in a novel by Hilary Mantel. A queen from this family died shortly after the birth of the future Edward VI and got engaged a day after the execution of her predecessor, Anne Boleyn. For 10 points, name this family of Henry VIII’s third wife, Jane.

For 10 points, name this family of Henry VIII's third wife, Jane.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Seymour [accept Jane Seymour; accept Edward Seymour; accept Thomas Seymour]

tossups.question: On All Hallows Day, the protagonist of this novel cries while reading a prayer book a month after burying his two daughters, one of whom was learning Greek and was buried with her copybook. The protagonist of this novel comforts a superior by interpreting his dream of his seemingly upset older brother returning in a white, fiery light. This novel begins in the town of Putney as the young protagonist is beaten by his father (*) Walter, who says, “So now get up.” The protagonist raises Rafe Sadler at Austin Friars in this novel, which introduces the antagonistic Stephen Gardiner and the protagonist’s boss Cardinal Thomas Wolsey. For 10 points, name this novel followed by Bring Up the Bodies and The Mirror and the Light in a trilogy about Thomas Cromwell, written by Hilary Mantel.

tossups.question: A character with this birth surname begins an affair with the low-status farmer William Stafford in Calais after the death of her first husband from an illness she calls “the sweat.” A novel narrated by a character with this surname both begins and ends with her watching the beheading of a family member, in between which she carries on a five-year affair facilitated by her brother George, who later impregnates their sister. A 2001 historical fiction bestseller title for a woman with this name was followed up by the similarly claustrophobic and melodramatic sequels The Queen’s Fool and The Virgin’s Lover. One writer who wrote about a woman with this surname took another to task for portraying that woman as a (*) seductress while idealizing her sister as a naive but caring mother. Mary, the elder sister of a woman with this surname, is the title “other girl” in a Phillipa Gregory bestseller. In another novel, the protagonist attempts to buy time for Cardinal Wolsey after he fails to annul the marriage of the romantic predecessor of a woman with this surname. For 10 points, Hilary Mantel’s Wolf Hall dramatizes the royal marriage of Anne, a woman with what surname?

tossups.question: In a short story collection titled for this action, Lola jealously writes "the less of her the better" about her anorexic older sister. The stories "The Heart Fails without Warning" and "Sorry to Disturb" appear in a short story collection titled for this action. In one novel, Dan is inspired to join to a plot to perform this action after his father is killed by a brick. In that same novel, Dan assumes the name of Roy Walsh and checks in at the 📚 Grand Hotel where Freya Finch works in preparation for performing this action. An attempt to perform this action is the subject of Jonathan Lee's novel, High Dive. A short story where this action takes place ends with a sniper declaring, (*) "Rejoice, fucking rejoice" and was rejected by the Daily Telegraph, despite its author having twice won the Booker for Bring Up the Bodies and Wolf Hall. For 10 points, name this action, successfully carried out by an IRA gunman in a Hilary Mantel short story, and almost carried out in real life during the Brighton Hotel Bombing.
tossups.answer: the attempted assassination of Margaret Thatcher [accept anything involving killing or shooting Margaret Thatcher]
tossups.answer_sanitized: the attempted assassination of Margaret Thatcher [accept anything involving killing or shooting Margaret Thatcher]

tossups.question: This character watches law students put on a play in which his patron offers “forty virgins” to anyone who will help him get unstuck in the mud. This man names his falcons after his deceased wife Liz and his daughters Anne and Grace, the latter of whom he often remembers wearing the angel wings he made for her. The biographer Diarmaid MacCulloch (“DER-mid muh-CULL-uck”) has defended the accuracy of this character’s portrayal. A novel opens with this character’s father Walter saying “So now get up” after beating him. In 2020, The Mirror and the Light will conclude a trilogy about this rival of Stephen Gardiner; the first novel in that series is titled for Jane Seymour’s estate and ends with Thomas More’s execution. For 10 points, Bring Up the Bodies and Wolf Hall by Hilary Mantel center on what adviser to Henry VIII?

bonuses.leadin: The original Globe Theater was set on fire by a cannon shot during a performance of this play, in which the Duke of Buckingham is executed on trumped-up treason charges brought by Thomas Wolsey. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this play, a collaboration between William Shakespeare and John Fletcher, whose title king secures a divorce from Queen Katherine in order to marry Anne Bullen.

Another depiction of the court of Henry VIII that debuted at the Globe Theater was this Robert Bolt play, which uses a recurring narrator called The Common Man and focuses primarily on Thomas More, the title character.

The story of Thomas Cromwell’s time in Henry VIII’s court is recounted by Hilary Mantel in a planned trilogy consisting of Wolf Hall, this second book, and the yet-to-be-released The Mirror and the Light.
answers: Henry VIII [or All is True; or The Famous History of the Life of King Henry the Eighth]
Bring Up the Bodies [do not accept “Bringing Up the Bodies”]
answers_sanitized: Henry VIII [or All is True; or The Famous History of the Life of King Henry the Eighth]
Bring Up the Bodies [do not accept "Bringing Up the Bodies"]

The story of Thomas Cromwell's time in Henry VIII's court is recounted by Hilary Mantel in a planned trilogy consisting of Wolf Hall, this second book, and the yet-to-be-released The Mirror and the Light.

bonuses.leadin: Several small roles in this play are assumed by a sporadic narrator called the Common Man. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play by Robert Bolt, which shows the downfall and execution of Sir Thomas More during the reign of Henry VIII.

In A Man For All Seasons, More says he would give even this figure the benefit of law, rather than "cut down every law in England" to defeat him. Blake said that Milton was "of [this figure's] party without knowing it" while writing Paradise Lost.
This Booker-winning 2009 historical novel by Hilary Mantel, titled for the Seymour family estate, ends with Thomas More's beheading. Its 2012 sequel, Bring Up the Bodies, also won a Booker.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about literary appearances of important people in the French Revolution, for 10 points each.
parts: Alexandre Dumas wrote a series of romances named for this queen, who is the subject of several prison rescue efforts after the death of her husband, Louis XVI, in The Knight of the Red House.

This writer directs a performance inside an asylum that ends with the murder of Jean-Paul Marat in a play by Peter Weiss. His own unfortunate protagonists include young women named Justine and Juliette.

This journalist totally flips out about getting guillotined in Hilary Mantel’s A Place of Greater Safety. His wife Lucille yells “Long live the king!” so that they can get guillotined together in Georg Büchner’s Danton’s Death.
answers: Marie Antoinette [or Maria Antonia Josepha Johanna; or Maria Antonia Josephina Johanna]
answers_sanitized: Marie Antoinette [or Maria Antonia Josepha Johanna; or Maria Antonia Josephina Johanna]
Donatien Alphonse Francois, Marquis de Sade

This journalist totally flips out about getting guillotined in Hilary Mantel's A Place of Greater Safety. His wife Lucille yells "Long live the king!" so that they can get guillotined together in Georg Buchner's Danton's Death.

bonuses.leadin: He described the hanging of a spy for Bernard-Rene de Launay, the governor of the Bastille, in his pamphlet, “The Streetlamp's Address to the Parisians”. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this schoolmate of Robespierre. This Dantonist argued for more moderate tactics in his paper Le Vieux Cordelier before he was executed during the Terror.

Desmoulins’ fellow Dantonist, Fabre d'Églantine, was executed when news broke that d’Eglantine had liquidated his shares in this company. This company reached its zenith under the leadership of Joseph-Francois Dupleix in the 1740s.

Camille Desmoulins was the subject of Hilary Mantel’s A Place of Greater Safety. Mantel may be better known for her Booker Prize-winning novels Wolf Hall and Bring Up the Bodies, set during the reign of this English king served by Cardinal Wolsey.
answers: French East India Company [or Compagnie française pour le commerce des Indes orientales or Compagnie Française des Indes or French Company of the Indies; prompt on partial answer]

bonuses.leadin: One member of this family kills a queen at the end of Bring Up the Bodies, the second novel in Hilary Mantel’s trilogy about him. For 10 points each:
parts: Give the surname of the man who “could not cease / in the inglorious arts of peace,” according to a “Horatian Ode” by Andrew Marvell. That figure is described in another poem as “guiltless of his country’s blood.”

The aforementioned reference to Oliver Cromwell occurs in this poem about a location where “some mute inglorious Milton may…rest.” It opens by stating that “the curfew tolls the knell of parting day.”
“Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard” is by this poet, who stated “where ignorance is bliss /’Tis folly to be wise” in his “Ode on a Distant Prospect of Eton College.”
